session_summary: Marcus directed a focused session to reconcile the MAR design-imbalance term (A)+(C) by settling the information convention (observed vs expected) and deriving the realized-information correction two independent ways (Claude analytic + a blind GPT-5.5 cross-model check), then de-provisionalizing @sec-termA. His role was to set the verification bar (cross-model agreement, blind packets), commission an independent audit, then enforce the audit's prose-honesty fixes (no over-claiming 'two ways agree' or the -0.22+Delta=-0.46 trap), and finally spec a handoff that scopes 'fully derived' to a later session with a standing cross-model protocol.

first; avoid heavy parallel R.

ai_side.evidence: I won't claim "two ways agree" until I've read and graded its derivation against WAY-1.
ai_side.note: Assistant withholds the agreement claim pending an actual graded comparison rather than asserting it.

ai_side.evidence: The naive value (+0.1→+0.3) is anchored ... high-variance to measure directly. The trustworthy low-variance objects are the paired gap and the analytic Δ_KM ... the naive value is the soft spot.
ai_side.note: Assistant self-flags the softest leg of the evidence (high-variance naive value) before the audit.

ai_side.evidence: if you run it alongside any heavy 20-core R job there's a documented segfault-under-contention risk — run it solo or with few cores.

ai_side.evidence: Want me to chase down #1 (pin the asymptote) or #2 (audit the fitted-vs-oracle exposition) next? Those are the two that would move the (A)+(C) from "settled in convention, anchored in value" to "fully closed."

ai_side.evidence: I set the default lean to (a)+pin because ... the exact (A)+(C) value isn't load-bearing for the IC headline — but the next session is told to surface that recommendation and let you redirect rather than commit to it silently.
